text: Burnout
To unwind
To recharge
Downtime
Overwhelmed
To set boundaries
To slow down
Self-care
Resilience
To bounce back
Sustainable habits
Work-life balance
To keep up with
I should have slowed down sooner.
I should have set boundaries far sooner.
I ought to have protected my downtime.
I should not have said yes to everything.
You had better slow down before you burn out.
You look exhausted, Pricila. Are you still trying to keep up with that impossible schedule?
I am, and I am completely overwhelmed. Honestly, I should have set some boundaries months ago.
You really had better slow down before this turns into burnout. When did you last have any real downtime?
I genuinely cannot remember. I ought to have protected my weekends, but I kept treating self-care as something to do later.
Start small. A few sustainable habits, a real evening to unwind. That is how you build resilience.
You are right. I will protect my downtime, recharge properly, and finally get my work-life balance back.

Honestly, last year nearly broke me. I kept up with everyone else, said yes to everything, and never once slowed down. By December I was completely overwhelmed and heading straight for burnout. Looking back, I should have set boundaries far sooner, and I ought to have protected my downtime instead of giving it away. What saved me was almost embarrassingly simple. I started to unwind in the evenings, I built a few sustainable habits, and I let myself recharge on the weekends. It took time, but I bounced back. If you are running on empty right now, take it from me: you had better slow down before your body forces you to.

People assume retirement is the moment you finally relax, but I have learned that resilience is a habit you build long before you stop working. For years I treated self-care as a reward I had to earn, something for after the work was done. That was a mistake. These days I see work-life balance differently. I protect my downtime the way I protect an important meeting. I unwind without guilt, I recharge before I am empty, not after. A slower pace has not made me less capable. If anything, it has made me steadier, calmer, and far better at bouncing back when life gets hard.
